title: TPO Single-Ply Roofing in Springfield, MO | Commercial Roofers of Springfield
metaDescription: TPO roofing for Springfield warehouses, retail, and clinics — mil thickness, seam welding, and wind-uplift fastening details most competing bids gloss over entirely.

html:
TPO Bids That Skip the Mil Thickness Question

TPO is the membrane we install most often on the big flat roofs around Springfield, from distribution buildings to strip retail. It is also the easiest membrane to underprice, because the number that drives long-term performance rarely shows up in bold print on the quote.

The Number Most Bids Bury: Mil Thickness

Forty-five mil, sixty mil, and eighty mil TPO all look identical on a roof the day they go down. The difference shows up five to ten years later, when hail impact, foot traffic near rooftop units, and UV exposure have had time to work on the membrane. A 45 mil sheet costs less per square, and a bid that quotes it without saying so is quoting a shorter functional life than a property manager may be expecting.

We spec thickness based on the roof's actual exposure — hail history for the area, rooftop traffic near HVAC and exhaust equipment, and how long the owner plans to hold the building. That number goes on the estimate in plain language, not buried in a product code.

Heat-Welded Seams and Why the Welder Matters

TPO seams are hot-air welded, and weld quality depends on machine speed, ambient temperature, and probe testing after the fact. A rushed crew running a welder too fast to hit a schedule produces seams that look fine and fail early, usually at the T-joints where three sheets meet.

We probe-test seams as we go rather than at the end of the job, so a bad weld gets caught and redone the same day, not discovered during the first heavy rain.

Fastening Patterns for This Region's Wind Exposure

Southwest Missouri sees straight-line wind gusts from severe thunderstorm lines and real tornado risk every spring, and a mechanically attached TPO roof lives or dies on its fastening pattern. A bid that quotes a generic fastener spacing without calculating the building's actual height, corner and perimeter zones, and exposure category is guessing at a number that matters in an eighty mile-per-hour gust.

We calculate uplift pressure per zone for the specific building and adjust fastener density at corners and edges accordingly, which is also where most wind-related membrane failures start.

Where TPO shows up most in this market, in rough order of how often we install it:

  • Big-box retail and distribution buildings with large uninterrupted membrane runs
  • Medical office buildings and outpatient clinics attached to larger hospital campuses
  • Multi-tenant strip retail centers with mixed rooftop HVAC loads
  • School and university buildings needing a reflective, low-maintenance membrane
  • Warehouse and light industrial roofs converting from an aging built-up system
  • Self-storage facilities where budget and wind performance both matter

Where TPO Shows Up Most in This Market

TPO covers a lot of ground here because it fits both budget and performance goals across very different building types, and it has largely replaced older ballasted and built-up systems as the default choice on new low-slope construction and full replacements alike.

The tradeoff worth knowing about going in: TPO's reflective white surface keeps rooftop temperatures down, which helps HVAC load in a market with humid Ozarks summers, but that same surface shows dirt and biological growth faster than a darker membrane, especially near tree lines or industrial exhaust sources.

What a Complete TPO Bid Should Include

Beyond membrane thickness and fastening, a complete bid states insulation R-value, cover board type, walkway pad locations near serviceable rooftop equipment, and manufacturer warranty terms in writing before any deposit changes hands.

If a bid leaves any of those out, ask for them in writing before comparing price. A lower number with fewer specifics is not automatically a worse deal, but you cannot tell without the detail, and change orders on a tear-off almost always run higher than the same item priced up front.

Questions We Get About TPO

Is TPO a good fit for a roof with a lot of rooftop equipment?

Yes, with walkway pads and properly flashed curbs at each unit. Traffic concentrated around service points is where TPO wears fastest, so we plan pad placement around the actual maintenance routes, past the equipment footprint alone.

How does hail affect a TPO roof?

Thicker membrane resists puncture and bruising better, but hail damage on TPO is often cosmetic unless the impact is severe enough to compromise the membrane surface. We inspect after major regional hail events rather than waiting for a leak to show up months later.

Can TPO be installed over an existing roof?

Sometimes, as a recover system with a cover board, if the existing roof passes a moisture scan and the building can carry the added weight and layer count under code. We check both before recommending it, and if the scan shows wet insulation under a large enough section, we will say so even when a recover would have been the cheaper quote to sell.

What warranty terms actually matter?

Whether it is a material-only warranty or a full system warranty covering labor, and whether wind speed coverage matches this region's actual exposure. A twenty-year warranty with a low wind speed rating is not much protection here.

How long does a typical TPO installation take?

It depends heavily on building size and whether it is tear-off or recover, and on weather windows — spring storm season can push a schedule several days at a time. We build weather contingency into the timeline up front instead of promising a date we might miss, and we sequence tear-off in sections so an occupied building is never left fully exposed overnight.
